## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BM
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
6	`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
7	`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; `0/1/2/3` for `BURE_VERBOSE`. These bare values
8	appear scattered across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps),
9	YAML (Cloud Build substitutions), and Rust (test fixtures), with no
10	cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its enum family.
11	This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace work,
12	prone to silent drift.
13	
14	There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
15	`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works
16	but uses a different shape from what this heat will adopt; harmonization
17	is part of the sweep.
18	
19	## Convention — locked
20	
21	Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:
22	
23	- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
24	- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
25	             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
26	             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
27	- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
28	             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
29	             `e` = environment, etc.)
30	- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
31	- `_<value>` — the canonical value name
32	
33	Concrete renames:
34	
35	| Variable          | Bare value | Sprue value     |
36	|-------------------|------------|-----------------|
37	| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE  | conjure    | `rbnve_conjure` |
38	| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE  | bind       | `rbnve_bind`    |
39	| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE  | graft      | `rbnve_graft`   |
40	| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE  | tether     | `rbnve_tether`  |
41	| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE  | airgap     | `rbnve_airgap`  |
42	| BURN_PLATFORM     | bubep_linux| `bunne_linux`   |
43	| BURN_PLATFORM     | bubep_mac  | `bunne_mac`     |
44	| BURN_PLATFORM     | bubep_windows | `bunne_windows` |
45	
46	Vessel-mode and egress-mode share `rbnve_` because both are vessel-regime
47	enums — single grep finds both clusters. This is a deliberate property
48	of the convention, not an accident.
49	
50	## Existing exemplar — `bubep_*` harmonize-or-grandfather
51	
52	`burn_regime.sh:44` already declares prefixed values, but with shape
53	`bubep_*` (5 chars, includes per-variable `p` discriminator) rather than
54	`bunne_*` (4 chars, no per-variable discriminator). Two coherent
55	positions; pick at migration time:
56	
57	- **Harmonize**: rename `bubep_*` → `bunne_*` in the same sweep. ~17 use
58	  sites in `bujb_*.sh`. Eliminates two-shape-cognitive-cost wart.
59	- **Grandfather**: leave `bubep_*` alone, apply new convention to
60	  newly-prefixed enums only. Cheaper, leaves asymmetry permanent.
61	
62	Lean toward harmonize per load-bearing-complexity discipline.
63	
64	## Scope — sweep covers all five language ecosystems
65	
66	| Language       | Where to look                                              |
67	|----------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
68	| Bash           | `Tools/buk/*.sh`, `Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `tt/*.sh`           |
69	| AsciiDoc       | `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` (regime + spec docs)           |
70	| Python         | `Tools/rbk/rbgja/*.py`, `Tools/rbk/rbgjv/*.py`             |
71	| Cloud Build YAML | `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE`, `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE` substitutions in `rbfd_*` and `rbfv_*` step assembly |
72	| Rust           | `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/*.rs` (test-fixture baseline strings)  |
73	
74	## Inventory needs
75	
76	Three classes per enum value:
77	
78	1. **Declaration sites** — `buv_enum_enroll` and `buv_gate_enroll` lines.
79	   Authoritative source of truth for the value set.
80	2. **Code use sites** — equality tests, case-statement arms, JSON
81	   literals, conditional dispatch. Mechanical to find.
82	3. **Comment / prose use sites** — bash/Rust/Python comments mentioning
83	   the value, AsciiDoc prose mentioning the value. Disambiguation is
84	   judgment work (see comment classification below).
85	
86	## Discovery recipe
87	
88	- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — declarations
89	- Per discovered value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/` filtered by
90	  extension for code sites
91	- Comment search per V:
92	  - `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b' Tools/` — bash/Python comments
93	  - `grep -rn '//.*\bV\b' Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/` — Rust comments
94	  - `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` — AsciiDoc prose
95	- Cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations against the discovered enum
96	  set to verify no enum was missed
97	- Cross-check `RB[SR]*` and `BU[S]*` regime spec `.adoc` files for
98	  prose mentions in spec descriptions
99	
100	## Comment classification — judgment work
101	
102	Bare-word grep on comments yields three classes:
103	
104	1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the graft
105	   pipeline" — referring to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave
106	   alone**; renaming makes the prose worse.
107	2. **Enum-value references** — "vessel mode is conjure", "if mode is
108	   bind" — referring to the actual variable value. **Migrate** to
109	   sprue value or use canonical reference like `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE =
110	   {rbnve_conjure}`.
111	3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`
112	   function names — the bare word appears as part of an identifier.
113	   **Leave alone**; identifier names are their own naming concern.
114	
115	The migration paces' work: walk the candidate list, classify each hit,
116	migrate class 2, leave classes 1 and 3.
117	
118	## Posture
119	
120	**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform
121	on execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place.
122	Single-sweep target. Operator pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all
123	crucibles quenched.
124	
125	**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`,
126	and `gauntlet` fixtures catch any bare-value site that wasn't migrated
127	because the enum-validation gate in `buv_enum_enroll` will reject the
128	non-prefixed value once the regime declaration is updated.
129	
130	## What done looks like
131	
132	- All bare enum values (`conjure`, `bind`, `graft`, `tether`, `airgap`,
133	  optionally `bubep_*`) replaced with sprue-prefixed values across all
134	  five language ecosystems
135	- `buv_enum_enroll` declarations updated to expect the new values
136	- All `rbrv.env` and any other regime files updated in place
137	- `regime-validation` fast fixture green
138	- `dockerfile-hygiene` fixture green (smoke proof of vessel-mode logic
139	  working with new values)
140	- `gauntlet` suite green end-to-end
141	- A single grep per sprue family (`grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`,
142	  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/`) returns the complete cluster
143	
144	## Out of scope for this heat
145	
146	- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes,
147	  JSON wire-format keys, etc.) — separate concern, separate heat
148	- Convention reformulation for `BURE_VERBOSE` integer values — bare
149	  ints aren't the same problem class
150	- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind
151	
152	## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)
153	
154	Likely sequence when this heat is mounted:
155	
156	1. Inventory pace — produce the full site listing across all five
157	   languages, classify comments, recommend cadence
158	2. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec
159	   section so future readers find it
160	3. Migration paces — cadence TBD (per regime, per variable, or per
161	   language slice; inventory feeds the decision)
162	4. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + grep audit
163	
164	Cadence and pace decomposition are mount-time decisions; do not
165	pre-cut paces from this paddock.

file.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BM

## Context

Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; `0/1/2/3` for `BURE_VERBOSE`. These bare values
appear scattered across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps),
YAML (Cloud Build substitutions), and Rust (test fixtures), with no
cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its enum family.
This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace work,
prone to silent drift.

There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works
but uses a different shape from what this heat will adopt; harmonization
is part of the sweep.

## Convention — locked

Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:

- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
             `e` = environment, etc.)
- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
- `_<value>` — the canonical value name

Concrete renames:

| Variable          | Bare value | Sprue value     |
|-------------------|------------|-----------------|
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE  | conjure    | `rbnve_conjure` |
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE  | bind       | `rbnve_bind`    |
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE  | graft      | `rbnve_graft`   |
| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE  | tether     | `rbnve_tether`  |
| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE  | airgap     | `rbnve_airgap`  |
| BURN_PLATFORM     | bubep_linux| `bunne_linux`   |
| BURN_PLATFORM     | bubep_mac  | `bunne_mac`     |
| BURN_PLATFORM     | bubep_windows | `bunne_windows` |

Vessel-mode and egress-mode share `rbnve_` because both are vessel-regime
enums — single grep finds both clusters. This is a deliberate property
of the convention, not an accident.

## Existing exemplar — `bubep_*` harmonize-or-grandfather

`burn_regime.sh:44` already declares prefixed values, but with shape
`bubep_*` (5 chars, includes per-variable `p` discriminator) rather than
`bunne_*` (4 chars, no per-variable discriminator). Two coherent
positions; pick at migration time:

- **Harmonize**: rename `bubep_*` → `bunne_*` in the same sweep. ~17 use
  sites in `bujb_*.sh`. Eliminates two-shape-cognitive-cost wart.
- **Grandfather**: leave `bubep_*` alone, apply new convention to
  newly-prefixed enums only. Cheaper, leaves asymmetry permanent.

Lean toward harmonize per load-bearing-complexity discipline.

## Scope — sweep covers all five language ecosystems

| Language       | Where to look                                              |
|----------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| Bash           | `Tools/buk/*.sh`, `Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `tt/*.sh`           |
| AsciiDoc       | `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` (regime + spec docs)           |
| Python         | `Tools/rbk/rbgja/*.py`, `Tools/rbk/rbgjv/*.py`             |
| Cloud Build YAML | `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE`, `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE` substitutions in `rbfd_*` and `rbfv_*` step assembly |
| Rust           | `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/*.rs` (test-fixture baseline strings)  |

## Inventory needs

Three classes per enum value:

1. **Declaration sites** — `buv_enum_enroll` and `buv_gate_enroll` lines.
   Authoritative source of truth for the value set.
2. **Code use sites** — equality tests, case-statement arms, JSON
   literals, conditional dispatch. Mechanical to find.
3. **Comment / prose use sites** — bash/Rust/Python comments mentioning
   the value, AsciiDoc prose mentioning the value. Disambiguation is
   judgment work (see comment classification below).

## Discovery recipe

- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — declarations
- Per discovered value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/` filtered by
  extension for code sites
- Comment search per V:
  - `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b' Tools/` — bash/Python comments
  - `grep -rn '//.*\bV\b' Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/` — Rust comments
  - `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` — AsciiDoc prose
- Cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations against the discovered enum
  set to verify no enum was missed
- Cross-check `RB[SR]*` and `BU[S]*` regime spec `.adoc` files for
  prose mentions in spec descriptions

## Comment classification — judgment work

Bare-word grep on comments yields three classes:

1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the graft
   pipeline" — referring to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave
   alone**; renaming makes the prose worse.
2. **Enum-value references** — "vessel mode is conjure", "if mode is
   bind" — referring to the actual variable value. **Migrate** to
   sprue value or use canonical reference like `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE =
   {rbnve_conjure}`.
3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`
   function names — the bare word appears as part of an identifier.
   **Leave alone**; identifier names are their own naming concern.

The migration paces' work: walk the candidate list, classify each hit,
migrate class 2, leave classes 1 and 3.

## Posture

**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform
on execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place.
Single-sweep target. Operator pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all
crucibles quenched.

**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`,
and `gauntlet` fixtures catch any bare-value site that wasn't migrated
because the enum-validation gate in `buv_enum_enroll` will reject the
non-prefixed value once the regime declaration is updated.

## What done looks like

- All bare enum values (`conjure`, `bind`, `graft`, `tether`, `airgap`,
  optionally `bubep_*`) replaced with sprue-prefixed values across all
  five language ecosystems
- `buv_enum_enroll` declarations updated to expect the new values
- All `rbrv.env` and any other regime files updated in place
- `regime-validation` fast fixture green
- `dockerfile-hygiene` fixture green (smoke proof of vessel-mode logic
  working with new values)
- `gauntlet` suite green end-to-end
- A single grep per sprue family (`grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`,
  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/`) returns the complete cluster

## Out of scope for this heat

- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes,
  JSON wire-format keys, etc.) — separate concern, separate heat
- Convention reformulation for `BURE_VERBOSE` integer values — bare
  ints aren't the same problem class
- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind

## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)

Likely sequence when this heat is mounted:

1. Inventory pace — produce the full site listing across all five
   languages, classify comments, recommend cadence
2. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec
   section so future readers find it
3. Migration paces — cadence TBD (per regime, per variable, or per
   language slice; inventory feeds the decision)
4. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + grep audit

Cadence and pace decomposition are mount-time decisions; do not
pre-cut paces from this paddock.

content.text:
## Groom: ₣BM — rbk-15-mvp-regime-sprues

**Status:** stabled · Created 260514 · **No paces enrolled yet**

### Heat shape (from paddock)

Migrate bare regime-enum values (`conjure`/`bind`/`graft`, `tether`/`airgap`, and the existing `bubep_*` platform values) to a **sprue-prefixed convention** so each value carries a cluster-discoverable marker tying it to its enum family. Today these bare words are scattered across bash, AsciiDoc, Python, Cloud Build YAML, and Rust with no grep-able linkage — mode renames are cross-language hand-search-and-replace, prone to silent drift.

**Convention (locked):** `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>`
| Variable | Bare → Sprue |
|----------|--------------|
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE | `conjure/bind/graft` → `rbnve_conjure` / `rbnve_bind` / `rbnve_graft` |
| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE | `tether/airgap` → `rbnve_tether` / `rbnve_airgap` |
| BURN_PLATFORM | `bubep_linux/mac/windows` → `bunne_linux/mac/windows` (harmonize) |

**Posture:** burn-bridges, no back-compat, single-sweep. `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` fixtures are the stragglers net.

### Open decisions the paddock flags

1. **`bubep_*` → harmonize or grandfather?** Paddock leans harmonize (~17 sites in `bujb_*.sh`), per load-bearing-complexity discipline. Not yet locked.
2. **Migration cadence** — per-regime, per-variable, or per-language slice. Explicitly deferred to inventory output.

### Suggested pace structure

The paddock sketches a likely sequence (deferred to mount-time). A natural slate:

1. **Inventory** — full site listing across all five languages; classify comment hits (phase-reference / enum-value / identifier-embed); recommend cadence
2. **Convention spec authoring** — formalize the sprue convention as a spec section so future readers find it
3. **Migration paces** — cadence TBD, fed by the inventory
4. **Final regression sweep** — gauntlet end-to-end + per-family grep audit

---

Since this heat is stabled with zero paces, grooming here is really about **deciding what to slate**. Before I propose concrete paces:

content.text:
## Inventory complete — ₣BM regime-sprue migration

I ran the full discovery recipe across all five ecosystems. The inventory both **confirms the paddock's locked scope** and **surfaces three findings the paddock didn't capture** — two of which are genuine scope decisions for you.

### Complete enum landscape (authoritative — from `buv_enum_enroll` declarations)

| Variable | Values | Family | Paddock status |
|----------|--------|--------|----------------|
| `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE` | `bind` `conjure` `graft` | vessel | ✅ locked → `rbnve_*` |
| `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE` | `tether` `airgap` | vessel | ✅ locked → `rbnve_*` |
| `BURN_PLATFORM` | `bubep_linux/mac/windows` | node | ✅ locked → `bunne_*` (harmonize) |
| `RBRN_RUNTIME` | `docker` `podman` | node | ⚠️ **not in locked table** |
| `RBRN_ENTRY_MODE` | `disabled` `enabled` | node | ⚠️ **not in locked table** |
| `RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE` | `disabled` `global` `allowlist` | node | ⚠️ **not in locked table** |
| `RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE` | `disabled` `global` `allowlist` | node | ⚠️ **not in locked table** |
| `BURE_VERBOSE` / `BURE_COLOR` | `0/1/2/3`, `auto/0/1` | env | explicitly out of scope (ints) |

The `rbtdrf_fast.rs` hits (`TEST_MODE`, `TEST_FEAT_A/B` = debug/release/on/off) are **synthetic fixtures testing the buv machinery generically** — not real regime enums. Out of scope.

### Magnitude (files touched, distinctive values)

| Value | bash | adoc | python | rust |
|-------|------|------|--------|------|
| `conjure` | 17 | 20 | 3 | 4 |
| `graft` | 11 | 14 | 3 | 1 |
| `tether` | 10 | 8 | 0 | 1 |
| `airgap` | 10 | 11 | 0 | 3 |

`bubep_*`: **41 sites / 7 files** (paddock estimated ~17 — undercount; the real figure includes BUS0 spec + `buwz_zipper.sh`). YAML count is 0 — Cloud Build carries the value through **Python** step-assembly (`vessel_mode == "conjure"` in `rbgjv02`, `rbgja03`), not literal YAML.

### Three findings beyond the paddock

**1. A fourth comment-class: coupled derived strings (leave bare).** The paddock's 3-class model (phase-ref / enum-value / identifier-embed) misses a case the egress sweep hits hard: `tether`/`airgap` appear as **GCP worker-pool suffix strings** — `RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_TETHER="-tether"` (`rbgc_Constants.sh:264`), pool paths in `rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh:90`, and vessel sigils `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`. These are *derived from* the egress value but live in resource-name space and **must stay bare**. So even a class-2 case-arm migration is not blind find/replace: the arm becomes `rbnve_tether)` while the pool string it maps to stays `-tether`.

**2. `RBRN_RUNTIME` likely cannot adopt the convention at all.** Its value is consumed as the **literal executable name**: `command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"`, `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect` (`rbrn_cli.sh:60`, `rbob_bottle.sh:98`). Renaming to `rbnne_docker` would break dispatch — you'd need a value→binary indirection layer, which is a different problem class. **Recommend OUT of scope** (or grandfather like a coupled string).

**3. The other RBRN enums are migratable but high-noise.** `disabled`/`enabled`/`global`/`allowlist` are pure case-arm comparisons (migratable in principle) but are common English words → grep is mostly noise, classification is heavy judgment. They'd roughly double the heat's effort for node enums the locked table never claimed.

### Recommended cadence (per-variable)

1. **Spec-authoring first** — formalize the convention *including the class-4 "coupled derived string stays bare" rule*, so migration paces have a written contract.
2. **Egress-mode pilot** (`tether`/`airgap`) — smallest distinctive pair, but entangled with pool suffixes → best stress-test of the class-4 distinction. Proves the pattern.
3. **Vessel-mode** (`conjure`/`graft`/`bind`) — largest, crosses into Python (Cloud Build) and handbook prose.
4. **`bubep_*` → `bunne_*` harmonize** — independent mechanical slice, 41 sites.
5. **Final sweep** — `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` + per-family grep audit.

Per-language slices are *not* recommended: the work clusters by variable (the class-4 entanglement is per-value), not by file type.

---

Two decisions shape how many paces this heat needs:

content.text:
Both choices land — **all RBRN enums in scope** (including the `RBRN_RUNTIME` indirection layer) and **harmonize `bubep_*`**. That meaningfully expands the heat beyond the paddock's locked table. Here's the resulting target and a proposed pace slate.

### Full target value table (convention `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>`)

| Variable | Regime letter | Bare → Sprue |
|----------|:---:|--------------|
| `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE` | v | `bind/conjure/graft` → `rbnve_bind` / `rbnve_conjure` / `rbnve_graft` |
| `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE` | v | `tether/airgap` → `rbnve_tether` / `rbnve_airgap` |
| `RBRN_RUNTIME` | n | `docker/podman` → `rbnne_docker` / `rbnne_podman` **+ indirection** |
| `RBRN_ENTRY_MODE` | n | `disabled/enabled` → `rbnne_disabled` / `rbnne_enabled` |
| `RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE` | n | `disabled/global/allowlist` → `rbnne_disabled` / `rbnne_global` / `rbnne_allowlist` |
| `RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE` | n | `disabled/global/allowlist` → (same `rbnne_*` cluster) |
| `BURN_PLATFORM` | n | `bubep_*` → `bunne_linux` / `bunne_mac` / `bunne_windows` |

Two consequences of the choices worth locking into the paddock:

- **`rbnne_disabled` is shared by three node enums** (ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS). Consistent with the convention's deliberate family-sharing property (same as `rbnve_` spanning vessel+egress) — uniqueness lives in the full quoin, the prefix scopes the regime.
- **`RBRN_RUNTIME` now requires a value→binary indirection** — every `${RBRN_RUNTIME} image inspect` / `command -v "${RBRN_RUNTIME}"` site must resolve `rbnne_docker` → the literal `docker` executable via a derived constant (e.g. `RBDC_RUNTIME_BIN`). This is **new code**, not a rename — the novel-risk pace of the heat.

### Proposed pace slate (6 paces)

1. **Convention spec authoring** — formalize the sprue convention as a spec section: full target table, the **class-4 "coupled derived string stays bare"** rule (pool suffixes `-tether`/`-airgap`, vessel sigils), and the `RBRN_RUNTIME` indirection design.
2. **Vessel-regime sweep** — `rbnve_*` for VESSEL_MODE + EGRESS_MODE (shared family, one grep). Pilot: proves the mechanical pattern and the class-4 distinction. Crosses bash/adoc/Python (Cloud Build) /Rust.
3. **Node-mode sweep** — `rbnne_*` for ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS (shared family). High English-word noise → heavy comment classification.
4. **`RBRN_RUNTIME` indirection + migration** — build the binary-mapping layer, then flip `docker/podman` → `rbnne_*`. Isolated novel-code pace.
5. **`bubep_*` → `bunne_*` harmonize** — 41 sites / 7 files, mechanical.
6. **Final regression sweep** — `regime-validation` + `dockerfile-hygiene` + `gauntlet` + per-family grep audit (`rbnve_`, `rbnne_`, `bunne_`).

content.text:
Holding — nothing mutated. Let me put the open questions on the table, most consequential first.

**1. Does forcing `RBRN_RUNTIME` into the convention earn its keep?**

This is the one I'd push back on before we commit. The project's own load-bearing-complexity principle says an element must close a gap between intent and behavior. The convention's payoff is *grep-cluster discoverability* — one `grep rbnne_` finds the whole node-enum family. But `RBRN_RUNTIME` is a 2-value enum whose values are *also literal executable names*, so adopting the convention means adding a permanent `rbnne_docker → docker` indirection layer at every dispatch site. We'd be paying a real runtime-indirection cost to buy discoverability for two values that are already trivially greppable (`RBRN_RUNTIME` itself is the marker).

Contrast with `ENTRY/DNS/ACCESS_MODE`: those values (`disabled`/`global`/`allowlist`) are *pure comparison tokens* with no second life as binaries — the convention there is free. The asymmetry is real. My honest read: the other three RBRN enums clearly earn the prefix; `RBRN_RUNTIME` is the one place the convention costs more than it returns. Worth a deliberate "yes, anyway" rather than sweeping it in by default — you may value family-uniformity (all node enums prefixed, no exceptions) over the indirection cost, which is a legitimate call. Just want it to be the call, not the drift.

**2. Should the vessel-regime sweep split?**

I bundled VESSEL_MODE + EGRESS_MODE into one pace because they share `rbnve_`. But egress (`tether`/`airgap`, ~10 bash files, no Python) is a clean small pilot, while vessel-mode (`conjure`/`graft`/`bind`, ~17 bash + 20 adoc + Python Cloud Build steps + handbook prose) is the heaviest single body in the heat. Folding them together means the pilot's "prove the pattern cheaply" value is lost. I'd lean toward **egress as its own pilot pace, vessel-mode separate** — even though they share the grep family, the effort and risk profiles differ by a lot.

**3. Ordering: novel-risk pace early or late?**

The `RBRN_RUNTIME` indirection is the only pace inventing new mechanism. Standard instinct is front-load uncertainty. But it's isolated to one variable — a late surprise there doesn't invalidate the mechanical sweeps. The paddock wanted egress-as-pilot first. I think: **egress pilot → then runtime indirection → then the rest**. Pilot establishes rhythm cheaply; runtime indirection gets attention while the team is warm but before the bulk grind.

**4. Is pool-suffix the *only* class-4 coupled string?**

The class-4 rule (coupled derived strings stay bare) rests on the egress→pool-suffix coupling I found. Before writing it into a spec, I'd want the inventory to confirm whether vessel-mode has analogous coupled strings — e.g. are `conjure`/`graft` baked into any resource names, GAR paths, or artifact filenames the way `-tether`/`-airgap` are baked into pool names? If yes, the rule generalizes cleanly; if egress is the only case, the rule might be over-stated as a "class" versus a one-off note.

Which of these do you want to work first? My suggestion is to settle #1 (it changes scope) and #4 (it changes the spec pace's content), since those two ripple into everything else.

message.content:
1) I accept your argument to keep 'docker' and 'podman' without prefix.  Do not force RBRN_RUNTIME
2) Okay to split paces
3) you may reorder as per your suggestion
4) Please do extend the inventory accordingly for this and any other uncomfortable things in your experience.

Process these, but don't write paces yet.  Beware we are wroking on BK in a parallel chat: I think that heat is largely orthogonal but it could interfere.

aiTitle: Process refactoring decisions for runtime configuration

content.text:
Extended inventory done. The deeper probe confirmed the class-4 rule generalizes **and** turned up the most uncomfortable thing in the heat — a deferred-detection hazard the gauntlet net doesn't cover.

### Class-4 rule generalizes (good)

Vessel-mode has the same value→derived-token coupling that egress has with pool suffixes:
- `RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_CONJURE="c"`, `_BIND="b"`, `_GRAFT="g"` (`rbgc_Constants.sh:161-164`). The derived token (`c`/`b`/`g`) stays; only the dispatch that *selects* it on `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE` migrates.

So "coupled derived string stays bare" is a real class, not an egress one-off. The spec pace can write it as a general rule with two witnesses (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes).

### The uncomfortable finding: Cloud Build crosses a detection gap

Vessel-mode's value flows across a substitution boundary into a **remote execution context**, and the gauntlet's local `regime-validation` net does **not** cover the far side:

| Stage | Site | Class |
|-------|------|-------|
| Regime declaration | `rbrv_regime.sh:44` | migrate |
| Hardcoded substitution literal | `rbfd:793 "conjure"`, `rbfv:353,370 "graft"` | migrate |
| Dynamic substitution source | `$zjq_vessel_mode` (rbfd/rbfv) | migrate |
| Cloud Build **step shell** comparison | `rbgjv01:11 [ "${_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE}" = "conjure" ]` | migrate |
| Cloud Build **Python** comparison | `rbgjv02:139`, `rbgja01/03` `vessel_mode == "conjure"` | migrate |
| Rust fixture baseline | `rbtdrf_fast.rs:886,894 export RBRV_VESSEL_MODE="conjure"` | migrate |
| adoc wire-value doc | `RBSAV:192`, `RBSAB:133` `(conjure, bind, or graft)` | migrate |

`regime-validation` (fast) catches the declaration↔Rust-fixture lockstep. But the **step-shell and Python comparisons run remotely during an actual conjure/graft build** — a missed site there fails silently until someone runs a real Cloud Build (the full ordain path), not in the fast or even crucible suites. **Vessel-mode is the one pace where "gauntlet green" is not sufficient proof** — it needs an actual conjure + graft cloud build as its acceptance gate.

### Three more judgment traps a naive grep misses

1. **Default-value defaults** — `z_mode="${z_mode:-conjure}"` (`rbfd:1153`). Class-2, but a `== "conjure"` grep skips it. Migration recipe must also grep `:-conjure`, `:-bind`, etc.
2. **adoc wire-value tables look like prose** — `(conjure, bind, or graft)` in `RBSAV`/`RBSAB` *documents the literal substitution value*, so it's class-2 (migrate), not class-1 prose. Easy to misclassify and leave stale.
3. **Rust noise is almost all class-3** — `rbtdro_onboarding.rs` is full of `"ordain-conjure"`, `"wrest-bind"`, `"ordain-airgap"` step labels and `rbev-graft-demo` sigils (leave). Only the two `export RBRV_VESSEL_MODE="..."` lines in `rbtdrf_fast.rs` actually migrate. The Rust crate rebuilds (`tt/vow-b`) as part of that pace.

### Noise ranking (classification effort, bash)

`conjure` 66 lines > `bind` 56 > `graft` 47 > `tether/airgap` ~10 each. Counterintuitively **conjure is the noisiest** (heavy phase/operation prose), and `bind` carries English-word noise (bind-mount, IP bind). The egress pilot (~10 clean lines each) really is the right cheap proving ground; vessel-mode is the heavy classification grind.

### ₣BK interference vector

Concrete collision zone: the **bubep→bunne harmonize** pace touches BUK files — `burn_regime.sh`, `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`, `bujb_cli.sh`, `bujp_preflight.sh`, `buwz_zipper.sh`, `BUS0`. If ₣BK is working anywhere in `Tools/buk/`, that's where we'd step on each other. The `rbnve_`/`rbnne_` RBK-side paces (vessel/egress/node) are in `Tools/rbk/` and should stay clear. Sequencing implication: **bubep-harmonize is the natural last pace anyway, and it's the one to coordinate with ₣BK before mounting.**

That's the full extended inventory. Nothing written, no state touched. Where do you want to take it from here — fold this into the paddock, start slating, or keep refining?

message.content: Help me decide how we should proceed.  I sense you have a strong understanding of the work and am confident you will do a good job when we start.  Should we write detailed pace dockets now?  There's guidance I think in your context to keep paces from getting too detailed, important when there is a big gap of time between the slating of the pace and the implementation of it.  I think we will do this work very soon, so perhaps the detail guidance is actually good this time: we won't make other edits (besides what BK is doing).  Your thoughts?  Feel free to groom BK if that helps you opine.

## Paces

### moorings-filesystem-rename (₢BKAAD) [rough]

## Character

Hard cutover. The atomic move: filesystem rename + trampoline literal flip + RBCC alias value-flip + test-fixture path updates, all in one commit.

## Goal

`rbmm_moorings/` exists with the new layout. Old `.buk/`, `.rbk/`, and `rbev-vessels/` directories are gone. Trampoline literal points at the new launcher path. `RBBC_*` aliases in RBCC now resolve to moorings paths. All tabtargets still execute.

### kit-literal-sweep (₢BKAAE) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical with light context judgment. Parallel-internal: two agents, one per kit subtree. Sweeps both literals and the transitional `RBBC_*` alias names; retires aliases at the end.

## Goal

No `.buk`, `.rbk`, or `rbev-vessels` literals survive in kit code under `Tools/{rbk,buk}/`. No `RBBC_*` references survive anywhere in kit code. RBCC's transitional aliases are removed. Every reference goes through a named RBCC or BUBC constant.

## Work

### Parallel dispatch

Spawn two sonnet agents working on disjoint trees:

- **rbk-agent** — sweep `Tools/rbk/**/*.sh` AND `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/**/*.rs` (Rust files embed shell strings referencing kit vars — see `rbtdrf_fast.rs` for the canonical example):
  - Replace `.buk`, `.rbk`, `rbev-vessels` literals with RBCC constants minted in ₢BKAAC.
  - Replace `RBBC_*` references with their RBCC equivalents (`RBBC_dot_dir` → `RBCC_moorings_dir`, `RBBC_rbrr_file` → `RBCC_rbrr_file`, etc.).
  - Decide per-site which RBCC constant fits (the moorings dir alone, a subpath joined from constants, or a more specific named constant).
- **buk-agent** — sweep `Tools/buk/**/*.sh`:
  - Replace `.buk`, `.rbk`, `rbev-vessels` literals with BUBC constants from `bubc_constants.sh`. Mint additional BUBC constants if a needed concept is absent.

Both agents work on disjoint trees — no merge conflicts. Each agent's wrap criterion is its own grep returning empty for both classes of survivors.

### Selection discipline

- If a site uses `.buk/X` where X is a stable subpath, prefer a single composed reference (`"${RBCC_moorings_dir}/X"`) over minting a one-off constant.
- If a site uses `.buk/X` where X is a kit-defined identifier already constant-bound, compose via both constants (`"${RBCC_moorings_dir}/${RBCC_launchers_subdir}"`).
- If a literal recurs three or more times for the same concept, mint a constant rather than scattering composed references.

### RBCC alias retirement

After both agents wrap, remove the transitional `RBBC_*` aliases from RBCC (added in ₢BKAAC). If any `RBBC_*` references genuinely survive outside the swept subtrees, surface in the wrap commit — operator decides whether to keep the aliases or expand sweep scope.

### Survivors

After both agents wrap:
- `grep -rE '\.(buk|rbk)|rbev-vessels' Tools/{rbk,buk}/` returns only constant-definition sites and documented exceptions.
- `grep -rn 'RBBC_' Tools/` returns nothing (or only documented exceptions).

If literals or `RBBC_*` names genuinely survive after the sweep, that's the docket's open RBK-locale question answering itself empirically: surface the count and pattern in the wrap commit message — ₢BKAAI may need to register a finding.

## Wrap criteria

- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` passes.
- Both grep families return expected sites only.

- `.buk/` → `rbmm_moorings/` (subdirs determine specific final path)
- `.rbk/` → `rbmm_moorings/` (subdirs determine specific final path)

### moorings-acceptance (₢BKAAJ) [rough]

## Character

Two-platform gauntlet exit. Operator-driven; failure triage is opus-grade — a failure here is a real cutover bug, not exploration.

## Goal

`tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh` passes on macOS and linux against the new layout.

## Work

Run sequentially, never concurrent:

- macOS branch off the ₣BK heat branch: `tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh`.
- linux branch off the ₣BK heat branch: `tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh`.

The gauntlet runs canonical-establish (depot regen) + qualification in one pass. Per-platform branches keep canonical-depot commits isolated.

### Triage on failure

- **Grammar bug** (literal that escaped the sweep, wrong constant) — fix inline, re-run.
- **Contract bug** (cwd assumption that escaped the audit, layout assumption in untested code path) — fix inline if mechanical, spin off a pace if architectural.
- **Genuine architectural surprise** — stop, surface, decide. The cutover is wide enough that something subtle could surface only under the full gauntlet.

### Wrap criteria

- Both platform runs green.
- Any inline fixes are themselves committed as `jjx_record` entries against this pace.

## Paces

### moorings-filesystem-rename (₢BKAAD) [rough]

## Character

Hard cutover. The atomic move: filesystem rename + trampoline literal flip + RBCC alias value-flip + test-fixture path updates, all in one commit.

## Goal

`rbmm_moorings/` exists with the new layout. Old `.buk/`, `.rbk/`, and `rbev-vessels/` directories are gone. Trampoline literal points at the new launcher path. `RBBC_*` aliases in RBCC now resolve to moorings paths. All tabtargets still execute.

## Work

### Inventory check (do this first)

The renames table below must account for every tracked file under the three old roots. Before moving, run `git ls-files .buk .rbk rbev-vessels` and reconcile against the table — any tracked file not covered is a finding to resolve here, not leave stranded. Two carve-outs:

- `.rbk/rbje_compose_probe.env` — classify before moving: consumer config (→ moorings) or kit machinery (→ `Tools/rbk/`, like `rbob_compose.yml` does via ₢BKAAI). It is deliberately NOT in the table below pending that call.
- `.rbk/rbob_compose.yml` and `Tools/rbk/rbmP_laterSecurityAudit.md` are ₢BKAAI — NOT this pace.

### File moves (use `git mv` so history follows)

- `.buk/launcher.<wb>_workbench.sh` (all kits) → `rbmm_moorings/rbml_launchers/launcher.<wb>_workbench.sh`. Includes the off-pattern `launcher_nolog.rbw_workbench.sh`, moved to match whatever dispatch form ₢BKAAB settled on. (Drop the `_workbench` segment only if ₢BKAAB's trampoline literal expects it dropped — verify against that literal, not this table.)
- `.buk/rbmu_users/` → `rbmm_moorings/rbmu_users/`
- `.buk/rbmn_nodes/` → `rbmm_moorings/rbmn_nodes/` (BURN profiles — the `rbmn_` nodes member; absent from the original table)
- `.buk/burc.env` → `rbmm_moorings/burc.env`
- `.rbk/rbrr.env` → `rbmm_moorings/rbrr.env`
- `.rbk/rbrp.env` → `rbmm_moorings/rbrp.env`
- `.rbk/rbrd.env` → `rbmm_moorings/rbrd.env`
- `.rbk/<nameplate>/` → `rbmm_moorings/<nameplate>/` (each nameplate dir with its `rbrn.env`, `rbnnh_*`, and any `workspace/`)
- `rbev-vessels/` → `rbmm_moorings/rbmv_vessels/`

### Trampoline literal flip

Edit `tt/z-launcher.sh` to point at the new launcher path per ₢BKAAB's exact form. Single literal edit.

### RBCC alias value-flip

₢BKAAC left transitional `RBBC_*` aliases in RBCC pointing at `.rbk/...`. Flip the root value so aliases now resolve to moorings paths:

- `RBBC_dot_dir`: `.rbk` → `rbmm_moorings`
- Composed aliases (`RBBC_rbrr_file`, `RBBC_rbrp_file`, `RBBC_rbrm_file`, `RBBC_rbrd_file`) auto-flip via composition.

Single-line edit in RBCC, atomically bound to the filesystem move — both must land together so existing kit code reads paths matching the new filesystem.

### Test-fixture path updates

Any test fixture that creates `.buk/`, `.rbk/`, or `rbev-vessels/` paths in its setup must be updated in this same pace so the test suite still functions:

- Sweep `Tools/rbk/rbtd/` and test infrastructure for setup code creating these paths.
- Update fixture setup to create `rbmm_moorings/`-shaped trees.
- Mechanical but bulky.

## Wrap criteria

- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` passes.
- A representative tabtarget from each colophon family (rbw, buw, jjw, vvw) executes correctly.

## Risk

Highest-risk pace in the heat. Recovery is `git revert` of the wrap commit. Keep the commit pure rename + literal + alias-flip + fixture-paths.

## Sources

- ₣A_/₢A_AAJ docket — Renames table, Final layout
- `tt/z-launcher.sh` (created in ₢BKAAB) — literal to flip
- `Tools/rbk/rbcc_Constants.sh` — RBBC aliases established by ₢BKAAC
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/` — test fixture surface

### kit-literal-sweep (₢BKAAE) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical with light context judgment. Parallel-internal: two agents, one per kit subtree. Sweeps both literals and the transitional `RBBC_*` alias names; retires aliases at the end.

## Goal

No `.buk`, `.rbk`, or `rbev-vessels` literals survive in kit code under `Tools/{rbk,buk}/`. No `RBBC_*` references survive anywhere in kit code. RBCC's transitional aliases are removed. Every reference goes through a named RBCC or BUBC constant.

## Work

### Parallel dispatch

Spawn two sonnet agents working on disjoint trees:

- **rbk-agent** — sweep `Tools/rbk/**/*.sh` AND `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/**/*.rs` (Rust files embed shell strings referencing kit vars — see `rbtdrf_fast.rs` for the canonical example):
  - Replace `.buk`, `.rbk`, `rbev-vessels` literals with RBCC constants minted in ₢BKAAC.
  - Replace `RBBC_*` references with their RBCC equivalents (`RBBC_dot_dir` → `RBCC_moorings_dir`, `RBBC_rbrr_file` → `RBCC_rbrr_file`, etc.).
  - Decide per-site which RBCC constant fits (the moorings dir alone, a subpath joined from constants, or a more specific named constant).
- **buk-agent** — sweep `Tools/buk/**/*.sh`:
  - Replace `.buk`, `.rbk`, `rbev-vessels` literals with BUBC constants from `bubc_constants.sh`. Mint additional BUBC constants if a needed concept is absent.

Both agents work on disjoint trees — no merge conflicts. Each agent's wrap criterion is its own grep returning empty for both classes of survivors.

### Selection discipline

- If a site uses `.buk/X` where X is a stable subpath, prefer a single composed reference (`"${RBCC_moorings_dir}/X"`) over minting a one-off constant.
- If a site uses `.buk/X` where X is a kit-defined identifier already constant-bound, compose via both constants (`"${RBCC_moorings_dir}/${RBCC_launchers_subdir}"`).
- If a literal recurs three or more times for the same concept, mint a constant rather than scattering composed references.

### RBCC alias retirement

After both agents wrap, remove the transitional `RBBC_*` aliases from RBCC (added in ₢BKAAC). If any `RBBC_*` references genuinely survive outside the swept subtrees, surface in the wrap commit — operator decides whether to keep the aliases or expand sweep scope.

### Survivors

After both agents wrap:
- `grep -rE '\.(buk|rbk)|rbev-vessels' Tools/{rbk,buk}/` returns only constant-definition sites and documented exceptions.
- `grep -rn 'RBBC_' Tools/` returns nothing (or only documented exceptions).

If literals or `RBBC_*` names genuinely survive after the sweep, that's the docket's open RBK-locale question answering itself empirically: surface the count and pattern in the wrap commit message — ₢BKAAI may need to register a finding.

## Wrap criteria

- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` passes.
- Both grep families return expected sites only.

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbcc_Constants.sh` — RBCC constants minted in ₢BKAAC, alias-retire site
- `Tools/buk/bubc_constants.sh` — BUBC constants
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs` — canonical example of Rust-embedded shell referencing kit vars
- ₣A_/₢A_AAJ docket — Symbolic-constant discipline section

### marshal-zero-and-proof-regen (₢BKAAF) [rough]

## Character

Update Marshal so the lifecycle of new consumers + release proof recognizes the new layout.

## Goal

`tt/rbw-MZ.MarshalZeroes` produces a clean blank tree with `rbmm_moorings/` (not `.buk/`/`.rbk/`). `tt/rbw-MP.MarshalProofs` validates against new layout and completes cleanly.

## Work

### Zero template regen

- Read `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` (Marshal Lifecycle).
- Find where it generates `.buk/` and `.rbk/` directory structures for fresh consumers.
- Update generation to produce the `rbmm_moorings/` layout per AAJ Final layout section.
- Reference RBCC constants from ₢BKAAC where they exist; literal path fragments fed back through constants.

### Proof workflow validation

- The Marshal proof repo workflow tests a real release into a blank tree.
- If proof creates or expects old paths in its scaffolding, update.
- Run `tt/rbw-MP.MarshalProofs.sh` end-to-end; must complete.

### Why this is one pace, not two

Zero template and proof workflow are tightly coupled — Marshal proof exercises what Marshal zero produces. Splitting risks a green proof that doesn't actually exercise the new layout. Single pace keeps them in lockstep.

### Wrap criteria

- `tt/rbw-MZ.MarshalZeroes.sh` produces an `rbmm_moorings/` tree.
- `tt/rbw-MP.MarshalProofs.sh` completes.
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` passes.

## Risk note

Bugs here have downstream blast radius into ₣BB (rbk-16-mvp-release-qualification, 48/57 done in muster) — verify proof workflow before wrap.

## Sources

- ₣A_/₢A_AAJ docket — Final layout section

### bcg-tabtarget-path-indirection-section (₢BKAAG) [rough]

## Character

Codification. Prose largely drafted in AAJ docket; integrate into BCG's voice and structure.

## Goal

BCG carries a "Tabtarget Path Indirection" subsection peer to "Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables." Future readers learn the trampoline pattern from BCG rather than tribal knowledge.

## Work

- Locate the "Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables" section in `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md`.
- Add a peer subsection titled "Tabtarget Path Indirection."
- Content based on AAJ docket's BCG codification section. Adapt for BCG's voice; align with BCG's CLI-as-Module-Gateway principle (sections 44-77).
- Key teaching points:
  - Single tabtarget (`tt/z-launcher.sh`) resolves moorings location.
  - Trampoline normalizes cwd to repo root before launcher dispatch.
  - All other tabtargets invoke the trampoline with a minted moorings-launcher *sprue* (`{owner}ml_{launcher-id}` — `rbml_*` for RBK-authored launchers, `buml_*` for the BUK launcher infra hosting other kits), never a bare colophon. The trampoline strips the `*ml_` prefix to recover the launcher-id.
  - The sprue keeps launcher dispatch tokens in the underscore-shaped Primary Universe, distinct from the hyphenated colophon universe — a name collision the bare-workbench-id form would have invited.
  - Moorings location is single-point-of-customization at the tabtarget layer (parallel to BURC at the CLI layer).
  - Workbenches start with deterministic cwd regardless of user invocation directory.

### Wrap criteria

- BCG renders cleanly.
- Cross-references to related BCG sections (load-bearing complexity, CLI as module gateway, dispatch-provided BURD_*) are coherent.
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` passes (defensive — should be a no-op for doc-only changes).

## Sources

- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md` — target file

- ₣A_/₢A_AAJ docket — BCG codification section, prose draft

### docs-vocabulary-sweep (₢BKAAH) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical with linked-term subtlety. Parallel-internal: two agents, one per doc subtree.

## Goal

No document teaches `.buk/`, `.rbk/`, or `rbev-vessels/` to a reader. AsciiDoc linked terms reflect the new vocabulary coherently.

## Work

### Parallel dispatch

Spawn two sonnet agents working on disjoint doc subtrees:

- **handbook-agent** — onboarding tracks and human-facing handbook content:
  - `Tools/rbk/rbh0/` (rbho/rbhp/rbhw families — onboarding CLIs)
  - Any handbook display strings, probe outputs, learner-facing examples
  - Tabtargets surfaced in onboarding flows (`rbw-O*` series)

- **specs-agent** — specifications and meta-docs:
  - `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBS*.adoc` family (start with RBS0, RBRN, RBSRR, RBSRP, RBSRV)
  - `README.md`, `CLAUDE.md`
  - Consumer CLAUDE: `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/CLAUDE.consumer.md`
  - AsciiDoc linked terms — attribute references, anchors, replacement text — must stay coherent. A linked term whose definition mentions the old path needs both the definition text AND any anchor/attribute name updated if those embed the old vocabulary.

### Substitution rules (both agents)

- `.buk/` → `rbmm_moorings/` (subdirs determine specific final path)
- `.rbk/` → `rbmm_moorings/` (subdirs determine specific final path)
- `rbev-vessels/` → `rbmm_moorings/rbmv_vessels/`
- `.buk/users/` → `rbmm_moorings/rbmu_users/`
- `.buk/launcher.*` → `rbmm_moorings/rbml_launchers/launcher.*`

Where prose teaches the directory structure as a concept (not just a path), update both the path and any prose that describes the old structure (e.g., "consumer config under `.buk/`" → "consumer config under `rbmm_moorings/`").

### Coherence

Two agents on disjoint trees, but both applying the same substitution rules. Substitution rule consistency is the docket's contract — if either agent invents a different substitution, both wraps need to align before this pace wraps.

### Wrap criteria

- `grep -rE '\.(buk|rbk)/|rbev-vessels' Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/ Tools/rbk/rbh0/ README.md CLAUDE.md` returns empty (or only documented exceptions).
- Spot-render an onboarding track (`tt/rbw-Occ.OnboardingConfigureEnvironment.sh` etc.) and read through; vocabulary reads natural.
- AsciiDoc linked terms resolve.
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` passes.

- `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBS*.adoc` — spec surface
- ₣A_/₢A_AAJ docket — Handbook and specs section

### kit-cleanup (₢BKAAI) [rough]

## Character

Tidy. Mechanical file moves + CLAUDE.md updates.

## Goal

Stranded items relocated to their proper homes. Acronym registry reflects the new `rbm*_` and `buml_` families and the dispatch-sprue scheme.

## Work

- `.rbk/rbob_compose.yml` → `Tools/rbk/rbob_compose.yml`. Kit machinery (crucible topology), not consumer-tunable. (If the rename pace already moved this, verify and skip.)

### CLAUDE.md acronym registration

Register the moorings naming in the repo-root `CLAUDE.md` File Acronym Mappings. Under the RBK subdirectory section, the `rbm*_` family:

- `rbmm_` — Moorings umbrella (the directory itself)
- `rbml_` — Moorings launchers (the shared launcher directory; holds every kit's launcher file)

Alongside (BUK-side, near the `bubc_` neighborhood), the `buml_` family:

Register the **dispatch-sprue scheme** itself where the tabtarget/colophon discipline is described: sprues take the form `{owner}ml_{launcher-id}` (`rbml_*` RBK-authored, `buml_*` BUK-hosted), are the tabtarget→trampoline dispatch tokens, and live in the underscore-shaped Primary Universe distinct from hyphenated colophons.

### moorings-acceptance (₢BKAAJ) [rough]

## Character

Two-platform gauntlet exit. Operator-driven; failure triage is opus-grade — a failure here is a real cutover bug, not exploration.

## Goal

`tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh` passes on macOS and linux against the new layout.

## Work

Run sequentially, never concurrent:

- macOS branch off the ₣BK heat branch: `tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh`.
- linux branch off the ₣BK heat branch: `tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh`.

The gauntlet runs canonical-establish (depot regen) + qualification in one pass. Per-platform branches keep canonical-depot commits isolated.

### Triage on failure

- **Grammar bug** (literal that escaped the sweep, wrong constant) — fix inline, re-run.
- **Contract bug** (cwd assumption that escaped the audit, layout assumption in untested code path) — fix inline if mechanical, spin off a pace if architectural.
- **Genuine architectural surprise** — stop, surface, decide. The cutover is wide enough that something subtle could surface only under the full gauntlet.

### Wrap criteria

- Both platform runs green.
- Any inline fixes are themselves committed as `jjx_record` entries against this pace.

aiTitle: Process refactoring decisions for runtime configuration

content.text:
Groomed ₣BK. It's the moorings-cutover heat — racing, 7 rough paces left (AAD–AAJ), all path-locale work: renaming `.buk/`/`.rbk/`/`rbev-vessels/` → `rbmm_moorings/` and sweeping path literals into constants. That gives me a concrete read on interference, and it actually answers your docket-detail question.

## The interference is real but specific

**Semantically orthogonal, maximal file-surface overlap.** BK changes *path literals*; BM changes *enum-value literals* — different concerns, and mostly *different lines* in the same files (BK won't touch `= "airgap"`; BM won't touch `.rbk/`). So true merge conflicts are unlikely. But:

- ₢BKAAE (kit-literal-sweep) rewrites **all of `Tools/rbk/**/*.sh` + `rbtd/src/*.rs`** — exactly BM's surface.
- ₢BKAAH (docs-vocabulary-sweep) rewrites **`Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBS*.adoc`** — including RBSAV/RBSAB, my vessel-mode wire-value tables.
- Sharpest single collision: **`rbtdrf_fast.rs`** — BK's canonical sweep example *and* the home of BM's `export RBRV_VESSEL_MODE="conjure"` fixture lines. Adjacent edits likely.

Net: not hard conflicts, but **continuous line-number churn** across BM's entire target set while BK races.

## My opinion: keep dockets thin even now

Your imminence argument is sound for *one* of the two reasons brevity exists — but not the other, and the BK finding is what tips it:

1. **Staleness rationale — NOT actually neutralized.** Your premise is "no other edits will land." But BK *is* landing edits, across BM's whole file surface. Any line numbers or site enumerations I bake in are stale-on-arrival — BK reintroduces precisely the rot the imminence argument assumes away. The one exception the stale-by-mount filter already grants — *file paths as entry points* — survives BK fine; *line:count* enumerations don't.

2. **Over-prescription rationale — timing-independent.** Pre-baking classifications (which of 66 `conjure` hits is class-2) freezes my *current, imperfect* judgment into prescription. My inventory found three judgment traps I'd otherwise have missed (default-values, adoc wire tables, Rust-noise). Baking a site list invites the mount agent to trust it instead of re-running the discovery — and a single missed class-4 coupling becomes a silent bug. The discovery *recipe* is more robust than the discovery *result*.

**So: not detailed dockets. Instead, put the depth where it survives.** JJK's own guidance — "depth belongs in the slate commit message, not the docket body" — fits this situation exactly:

- **Paddock** carries the durable analysis: the full convention table, the scope decision (RBRN_RUNTIME out / others in / bubep harmonize), the class-4 rule *with both witnesses* (pool suffixes, hallmark prefixes), the **Cloud Build detection-gap** finding, and a heat-shape note on the BK file-surface overlap. This is the high-value capture — it's what I'd lose otherwise.
- **Dockets** stay thin: goal + boundary + acceptance gate + a *discovery recipe* (grep patterns) + the judgment-trap warnings as `## Character` notes. The one piece of genuine per-pace detail worth pinning is **vessel-mode's acceptance gate: an actual conjure + graft cloud build**, because "gauntlet green" provably doesn't cover the remote step-shell/Python comparisons. That's durable character, not a stale site list.
- **Slate commit messages** carry the rich per-pace findings when we enroll.

This gets you everything the detailed-docket instinct wants (nothing lost, ready to implement immediately) without the rot, and it's robust to BK churning underneath us.

## Two sequencing notes (independent of docket detail)

- The **bubep→bunne harmonize** pace is the one place BM and BK *semantically* collide — both edit BUK files (`burn_regime.sh`, `bujb_*`, `buwz_zipper.sh`, `BUS0`). Put it **last** and coordinate with BK before mounting it specifically.
- For the RBK paces, recommend **git-pull discipline between commits** rather than hard serialization — different lines means we can interleave, but stay current so line churn doesn't surprise us.

If you buy this, the natural next move is: **amend the paddock with the analysis, then slate the 6 paces as thin dockets.** I'd do the paddock first (it's the durable capture and the dockets lean on it). Want me to draft the paddock amendment for your review before anything's written?

message.content: One choice I have is to go finish BK and then come back to this chat once it is done.  Would you find this preferable?  Sometimes slating thin dockets then revising them seems to produce good results, but that isn't a scientific finding on my part...

content.text:
Yes — I'd genuinely prefer you finish BK first before we *implement* BM. But I'd separate two things you've bundled, because they have different answers.

**Implementation: wait for BK.** Two tree-wide sweeps over the same `Tools/rbk/**` files, driven by a human juggling two chats, is the one avoidable-risk scenario here. Even though the *lines* differ (path literals vs. enum values), letting BK land gives BM a settled substrate: no line-number churn, no git-pull dance, no reasoning about a file that's moving under me. BK is the active heat and "very soon" is cheap to wait out. The payoff is real and the cost is near zero.

**Capture: do it now, in this chat.** This is where your instinct and mine converge. The expensive, perishable thing isn't the dockets — it's the *analysis*: the convention table, the class-4 rule with both witnesses, the Cloud Build detection-gap, the three judgment traps, the noise ranking, the scope decisions you just made. That belongs in the **paddock**, and the paddock is BK-proof by construction (it names shape, not line numbers). If we close this chat without amending the paddock, a future mount agent re-derives all of it from scratch. So: amend the paddock now regardless of what we decide about slating.

**On your thin-slate-then-revise intuition — it's not unscientific, it's structurally sound, and here's why it works:** a thin docket slated now is a *durable anchor* (coronet + intent), and the revision-at-mount is where *accuracy* gets injected — exactly when the tree is settled and detail is fresh. You're unconsciously respecting the staleness boundary: anchor survives, detail is mount-time. It's the same principle as "depth in the commit message, not the docket body," just expressed as a two-step workflow. So your habit and my recommendation are the same shape.

Given that, my actual preference for sequencing the *capture*:

- **Amend the paddock now** (this chat) — non-negotiable, it's the real value.
- **Defer slating to your post-BK return** — *slightly* better than slating now, because the pace boundaries will be marginally better-informed against the settled tree (e.g., I can confirm BK's constant-extraction didn't reshape any vessel-mode comparison sites). The marginal cost of slating-then-revising would be re-reading inert paces; the marginal cost of paddock-then-slate-fresh is nearly nil since the paddock did the thinking.

So: **finish BK, but let me write the paddock amendment first so this chat's analysis is banked.** When you come back post-BK, mounting BM becomes "read paddock → re-validate inventory against settled tree (shape holds, line numbers refresh) → slate 6 thin dockets → execute" — fast, because the hard thinking is already in the paddock.

One caveat on the paddock write itself: BK is racing in a parallel chat under a different officium, and `jjx_paddock` commits. I'd be committing only the BM paddock file, which BK won't touch — clean and orthogonal. But if you'd rather have zero concurrent commits while BK is mid-pace, I can hold the paddock draft as text here and you commit it after BK wraps. Your call.

Want me to draft the paddock amendment now — committed, or as held text for you to review first?

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BM
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
6	`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
7	`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; `0/1/2/3` for `BURE_VERBOSE`. These bare values
8	appear scattered across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps),
9	YAML (Cloud Build substitutions), and Rust (test fixtures), with no
10	cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its enum family.
11	This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace work,
12	prone to silent drift.
13	
14	There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
15	`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works
16	but uses a different shape from what this heat will adopt; harmonization
17	is part of the sweep.
18	
19	## Convention — locked
20	
21	Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:
22	
23	- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
24	- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
25	             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
26	             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
27	- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
28	             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
29	             `e` = environment, etc.)
30	- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
31	- `_<value>` — the canonical value name
32	
33	Concrete renames:
34	
35	| Variable          | Bare value | Sprue value     |
36	|-------------------|------------|-----------------|
37	| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE  | conjure    | `rbnve_conjure` |
38	| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE  | bind       | `rbnve_bind`    |
39	| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE  | graft      | `rbnve_graft`   |
40	| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE  | tether     | `rbnve_tether`  |
41	| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE  | airgap     | `rbnve_airgap`  |
42	| BURN_PLATFORM     | bubep_linux| `bunne_linux`   |
43	| BURN_PLATFORM     | bubep_mac  | `bunne_mac`     |
44	| BURN_PLATFORM     | bubep_windows | `bunne_windows` |
45	
46	Vessel-mode and egress-mode share `rbnve_` because both are vessel-regime
47	enums — single grep finds both clusters. This is a deliberate property
48	of the convention, not an accident.
49	
50	## Existing exemplar — `bubep_*` harmonize-or-grandfather
51	
52	`burn_regime.sh:44` already declares prefixed values, but with shape
53	`bubep_*` (5 chars, includes per-variable `p` discriminator) rather than
54	`bunne_*` (4 chars, no per-variable discriminator). Two coherent
55	positions; pick at migration time:
56	
57	- **Harmonize**: rename `bubep_*` → `bunne_*` in the same sweep. ~17 use
58	  sites in `bujb_*.sh`. Eliminates two-shape-cognitive-cost wart.
59	- **Grandfather**: leave `bubep_*` alone, apply new convention to
60	  newly-prefixed enums only. Cheaper, leaves asymmetry permanent.
61	
62	Lean toward harmonize per load-bearing-complexity discipline.
63	
64	## Scope — sweep covers all five language ecosystems
65	
66	| Language       | Where to look                                              |
67	|----------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
68	| Bash           | `Tools/buk/*.sh`, `Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `tt/*.sh`           |
69	| AsciiDoc       | `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` (regime + spec docs)           |
70	| Python         | `Tools/rbk/rbgja/*.py`, `Tools/rbk/rbgjv/*.py`             |
71	| Cloud Build YAML | `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE`, `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE` substitutions in `rbfd_*` and `rbfv_*` step assembly |
72	| Rust           | `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/*.rs` (test-fixture baseline strings)  |
73	
74	## Inventory needs
75	
76	Three classes per enum value:
77	
78	1. **Declaration sites** — `buv_enum_enroll` and `buv_gate_enroll` lines.
79	   Authoritative source of truth for the value set.
80	2. **Code use sites** — equality tests, case-statement arms, JSON
81	   literals, conditional dispatch. Mechanical to find.
82	3. **Comment / prose use sites** — bash/Rust/Python comments mentioning
83	   the value, AsciiDoc prose mentioning the value. Disambiguation is
84	   judgment work (see comment classification below).
85	
86	## Discovery recipe
87	
88	- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — declarations
89	- Per discovered value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/` filtered by
90	  extension for code sites
91	- Comment search per V:
92	  - `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b' Tools/` — bash/Python comments
93	  - `grep -rn '//.*\bV\b' Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/` — Rust comments
94	  - `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` — AsciiDoc prose
95	- Cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations against the discovered enum
96	  set to verify no enum was missed
97	- Cross-check `RB[SR]*` and `BU[S]*` regime spec `.adoc` files for
98	  prose mentions in spec descriptions
99	
100	## Comment classification — judgment work
101	
102	Bare-word grep on comments yields three classes:
103	
104	1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the graft
105	   pipeline" — referring to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave
106	   alone**; renaming makes the prose worse.
107	2. **Enum-value references** — "vessel mode is conjure", "if mode is
108	   bind" — referring to the actual variable value. **Migrate** to
109	   sprue value or use canonical reference like `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE =
110	   {rbnve_conjure}`.
111	3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`
112	   function names — the bare word appears as part of an identifier.
113	   **Leave alone**; identifier names are their own naming concern.
114	
115	The migration paces' work: walk the candidate list, classify each hit,
116	migrate class 2, leave classes 1 and 3.
117	
118	## Posture
119	
120	**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform
121	on execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place.
122	Single-sweep target. Operator pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all
123	crucibles quenched.
124	
125	**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`,
126	and `gauntlet` fixtures catch any bare-value site that wasn't migrated
127	because the enum-validation gate in `buv_enum_enroll` will reject the
128	non-prefixed value once the regime declaration is updated.
129	
130	## What done looks like
131	
132	- All bare enum values (`conjure`, `bind`, `graft`, `tether`, `airgap`,
133	  optionally `bubep_*`) replaced with sprue-prefixed values across all
134	  five language ecosystems
135	- `buv_enum_enroll` declarations updated to expect the new values
136	- All `rbrv.env` and any other regime files updated in place
137	- `regime-validation` fast fixture green
138	- `dockerfile-hygiene` fixture green (smoke proof of vessel-mode logic
139	  working with new values)
140	- `gauntlet` suite green end-to-end
141	- A single grep per sprue family (`grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`,
142	  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/`) returns the complete cluster
143	
144	## Out of scope for this heat
145	
146	- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes,
147	  JSON wire-format keys, etc.) — separate concern, separate heat
148	- Convention reformulation for `BURE_VERBOSE` integer values — bare
149	  ints aren't the same problem class
150	- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind
151	
152	## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)
153	
154	Likely sequence when this heat is mounted:
155	
156	1. Inventory pace — produce the full site listing across all five
157	   languages, classify comments, recommend cadence
158	2. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec
159	   section so future readers find it
160	3. Migration paces — cadence TBD (per regime, per variable, or per
161	   language slice; inventory feeds the decision)
162	4. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + grep audit
163	
164	Cadence and pace decomposition are mount-time decisions; do not
165	pre-cut paces from this paddock.
166

# jjezs_paddock ₣BM

## Context

Regime enums in this project carry their values as bare English words —
`conjure`, `bind`, `graft` for `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE`; `tether`, `airgap` for
`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`; `0/1/2/3` for `BURE_VERBOSE`. These bare values
appear scattered across bash, AsciiDoc, Python (Cloud Build steps),
YAML (Cloud Build substitutions), and Rust (test fixtures), with no
cluster-discoverable marker tying any one value to its enum family.
This makes mode renames cross-language hand-search-and-replace work,
prone to silent drift.

There is one prior in-tree exemplar of the prefixed-value approach:
`bubep_linux | bubep_mac | bubep_windows` for `BURN_PLATFORM`. It works
but uses a different shape from what this heat will adopt; harmonization
is part of the sweep.

## Convention — locked

Pattern: `<proj><n><regime><e>_<value>` where:

- `<proj>` — project prefix (`rb` for Recipe Bottle, `bu` for BUK)
- `<n>`    — single letter signaling "this is a regime namespace value"
             (chosen `n` for "namespace", contrasting `r` which is taken
             for the regime-variable family `rbr*`/`bur*`)
- `<regime>` — single letter mirroring the regime family
             (`v` = vessel, `n` = node, `c` = config, `s` = station,
             `e` = environment, etc.)
- `<e>`    — single letter signaling "this is an enum value"
- `_<value>` — the canonical value name

Concrete renames:

| Variable          | Bare value | Sprue value     |
|-------------------|------------|-----------------|
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE  | conjure    | `rbnve_conjure` |
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE  | bind       | `rbnve_bind`    |
| RBRV_VESSEL_MODE  | graft      | `rbnve_graft`   |
| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE  | tether     | `rbnve_tether`  |
| RBRV_EGRESS_MODE  | airgap     | `rbnve_airgap`  |
| BURN_PLATFORM     | bubep_linux| `bunne_linux`   |
| BURN_PLATFORM     | bubep_mac  | `bunne_mac`     |
| BURN_PLATFORM     | bubep_windows | `bunne_windows` |

Vessel-mode and egress-mode share `rbnve_` because both are vessel-regime
enums — single grep finds both clusters. This is a deliberate property
of the convention, not an accident.

## Existing exemplar — `bubep_*` harmonize-or-grandfather

`burn_regime.sh:44` already declares prefixed values, but with shape
`bubep_*` (5 chars, includes per-variable `p` discriminator) rather than
`bunne_*` (4 chars, no per-variable discriminator). Two coherent
positions; pick at migration time:

- **Harmonize**: rename `bubep_*` → `bunne_*` in the same sweep. ~17 use
  sites in `bujb_*.sh`. Eliminates two-shape-cognitive-cost wart.
- **Grandfather**: leave `bubep_*` alone, apply new convention to
  newly-prefixed enums only. Cheaper, leaves asymmetry permanent.

Lean toward harmonize per load-bearing-complexity discipline.

## Scope — sweep covers all five language ecosystems

| Language       | Where to look                                              |
|----------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| Bash           | `Tools/buk/*.sh`, `Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`, `tt/*.sh`           |
| AsciiDoc       | `Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` (regime + spec docs)           |
| Python         | `Tools/rbk/rbgja/*.py`, `Tools/rbk/rbgjv/*.py`             |
| Cloud Build YAML | `_RBGA_VESSEL_MODE`, `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE` substitutions in `rbfd_*` and `rbfv_*` step assembly |
| Rust           | `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/*.rs` (test-fixture baseline strings)  |

## Inventory needs

Three classes per enum value:

1. **Declaration sites** — `buv_enum_enroll` and `buv_gate_enroll` lines.
   Authoritative source of truth for the value set.
2. **Code use sites** — equality tests, case-statement arms, JSON
   literals, conditional dispatch. Mechanical to find.
3. **Comment / prose use sites** — bash/Rust/Python comments mentioning
   the value, AsciiDoc prose mentioning the value. Disambiguation is
   judgment work (see comment classification below).

## Discovery recipe

- `grep -rn 'buv_enum_enroll' Tools/buk/ Tools/rbk/` — declarations
- Per discovered value V: `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/` filtered by
  extension for code sites
- Comment search per V:
  - `grep -rn '#.*\bV\b' Tools/` — bash/Python comments
  - `grep -rn '//.*\bV\b' Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/` — Rust comments
  - `grep -rn '\bV\b' Tools/*/vov_veiled/*.adoc` — AsciiDoc prose
- Cross-walk `buv_gate_enroll` invocations against the discovered enum
  set to verify no enum was missed
- Cross-check `RB[SR]*` and `BU[S]*` regime spec `.adoc` files for
  prose mentions in spec descriptions

## Comment classification — judgment work

Bare-word grep on comments yields three classes:

1. **Phase / concept references** — "the conjure phase", "the graft
   pipeline" — referring to the workflow, not the enum value. **Leave
   alone**; renaming makes the prose worse.
2. **Enum-value references** — "vessel mode is conjure", "if mode is
   bind" — referring to the actual variable value. **Migrate** to
   sprue value or use canonical reference like `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE =
   {rbnve_conjure}`.
3. **Identifier embeds** — `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `rbho_conjure_*`
   function names — the bare word appears as part of an identifier.
   **Leave alone**; identifier names are their own naming concern.

The migration paces' work: walk the candidate list, classify each hit,
migrate class 2, leave classes 1 and 3.

## Posture

**Burn-bridges migration.** No backwards compatibility. Depots reform
on execution, vessels re-charge, regime files get edited in place.
Single-sweep target. Operator pre-flight: `rbw-MZ` before sweep, all
crucibles quenched.

**Deep-test stragglers.** `regime-validation`, `dockerfile-hygiene`,
and `gauntlet` fixtures catch any bare-value site that wasn't migrated
because the enum-validation gate in `buv_enum_enroll` will reject the
non-prefixed value once the regime declaration is updated.

## What done looks like

- All bare enum values (`conjure`, `bind`, `graft`, `tether`, `airgap`,
  optionally `bubep_*`) replaced with sprue-prefixed values across all
  five language ecosystems
- `buv_enum_enroll` declarations updated to expect the new values
- All `rbrv.env` and any other regime files updated in place
- `regime-validation` fast fixture green
- `dockerfile-hygiene` fixture green (smoke proof of vessel-mode logic
  working with new values)
- `gauntlet` suite green end-to-end
- A single grep per sprue family (`grep -rn rbnve_ Tools/`,
  `grep -rn bunne_ Tools/`) returns the complete cluster

## Out of scope for this heat

- Sprue convention applied to non-regime-enum domains (status codes,
  JSON wire-format keys, etc.) — separate concern, separate heat
- Convention reformulation for `BURE_VERBOSE` integer values — bare
  ints aren't the same problem class
- Backwards-compatibility shims of any kind

## Pace shape (deferred to mount-time)

Likely sequence when this heat is mounted:

1. Inventory pace — produce the full site listing across all five
   languages, classify comments, recommend cadence
2. Convention spec authoring — formalize the convention as a spec
   section so future readers find it
3. Migration paces — cadence TBD (per regime, per variable, or per
   language slice; inventory feeds the decision)
4. Final regression sweep — gauntlet end-to-end + grep audit

Cadence and pace decomposition are mount-time decisions; do not
pre-cut paces from this paddock.
